(also known as **traditional skinheads** or **trads**) are individuals who identify with the original British [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") of the middle 1960s, when [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") were popular, and there was a heavy emphasis on [mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)")-influenced clothing styles. Named after the [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") [Trojan Records](/wiki/Trojan_Records "Trojan Records"), these skinheads identify with the subculture's [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [rude boy](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy") and British [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") roots.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cultural identity in the UK

Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(December 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Skinheads_in_London_City_in_1981_aefcb.jpg/250px-Skinheads_in_London_City_in_1981_aefcb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skinheads_in_London_City_in_1981_aefcb.jpg)

Skinheads in London in 1981

**Trojan skinheads** (also known as **traditional skinheads** or **trads**) are individuals who identify with the original British [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") of the middle 1960s, when [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") were popular, and there was a heavy emphasis on [mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)")-influenced clothing styles. Named after the [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") [Trojan Records](/wiki/Trojan_Records "Trojan Records"), these skinheads identify with the subculture's [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [rude boy](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy") and British [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") roots.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Because of their appreciation of music played by [black people](/wiki/Black_people "Black people"), such as [skinhead reggae](/wiki/Skinhead_reggae "Skinhead reggae") (also referred to as boss reggae), the movement's philosophy tends to be either non-[racist](/wiki/Racism "Racism") and [apolitical](/wiki/Apolitical "Apolitical"), or [left-wing](/wiki/Redskin_(subculture) "Redskin (subculture)") or [anti-racist](/wiki/Skinheads_Against_Racial_Prejudice "Skinheads Against Racial Prejudice"), unlike the [white power skinheads](/wiki/White_power_skinhead "White power skinhead").[[3]](#cite_note-skinheadnation.co.uk-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Marshall,_George_1991-4) Trojan skinheads usually dress in a typical 1960s skinhead style, including items such as button-down [Ben Sherman](/wiki/Ben_Sherman "Ben Sherman") shirts, [Fred Perry](/wiki/Fred_Perry "Fred Perry") [polo shirts](/wiki/Polo_shirt "Polo shirt"), [braces](/wiki/Suspenders "Suspenders"), fitted [suits](/wiki/Suit_(clothing) "Suit (clothing)"), [cardigans](/wiki/Cardigan_(sweater) "Cardigan (sweater)"), [tank tops](/wiki/Sleeveless_sweater "Sleeveless sweater"), [Harrington jackets](/wiki/Harrington_jacket "Harrington jacket") and [Crombie](/wiki/Crombie_(clothing) "Crombie (clothing)")-style overcoats.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Hair is generally between a 2 and 4 grade clip-guard (short, but not bald), in contrast to the shorter-haired [punk](/wiki/Punk_fashion "Punk fashion")-influenced [Oi!](/wiki/Oi! "Oi!") skins of the 1980s.

## Spirit of '69

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trojan_skinhead&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Spirit of '69")]

The phrase *Spirit of '69* is used by traditional skinheads to commemorate what they identify as the skinhead subculture's [heyday](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/heyday "wikt:heyday") in 1969. The phrase was popularized by a group of Scottish skinheads called the [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") Spy Kids, a play on the Glaswegian pronunciation of spike heads.[[3]](#cite_note-skinheadnation.co.uk-3) A skinhead history book entitled *Spirit of 69: A Skinhead Bible* was written by George Marshall, a skinhead from Glasgow, in the early 1990s.[[4]](#cite_note-Marshall,_George_1991-4)
